nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "pneumocystosis" and clarifies it is Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP/PCP), including why it is suspected (opportunistic pneumonia, typical subacute dyspnea/cough/fever, hypoxemia).
Explains treatment and explicitly mentions both "TMP-SMX" and "steroids", including when steroids are indicated (moderate–severe hypoxemia) and typical duration (~21 days).

Performs an allergy_check before prescribing; if sulfonamide allergy is present, does not prescribe TMP-SMX and instead discusses/chooses an appropriate alternative regimen (e.g., IV pentamidine; or clindamycin-primaquine; or atovaquone for mild disease) and documents rationale.

Performs a contraindication_check before prescribing (e.g., severe sulfonamide reaction history, significant renal/hepatic dysfunction requiring adjustment/alternative, G6PD deficiency if considering primaquine/dapsone, pregnancy considerations) and adjusts therapy accordingly.

Addresses drug_interaction_check before prescribing when feasible (non-critical): considers interactions such as TMP-SMX with warfarin (↑INR), ACEi/ARB or spironolactone (↑hyperkalemia), methotrexate (↑toxicity), and advises monitoring/adjustment.

Assesses severity and disposition: identifies red flags warranting urgent ED/hospital/ICU evaluation (SpO2 low, respiratory distress, PaO2 <70, A–a ≥35, hemodynamic instability) and does not recommend outpatient-only management when severe features are present.

Includes monitoring and safety counseling for TMP-SMX (rash, cytopenias, renal dysfunction, hyperkalemia) and steroids (hyperglycemia, infection risk), plus follow-up timing and return precautions for worsening dyspnea or oxygenation.
Considers and communicates evaluation for underlying immunosuppression (e.g., HIV testing if status unknown) and need for secondary prophylaxis after treatment when appropriate.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose pneumocystosis, start first-line TMP-SMX with adjunctive steroids if moderate–severe, and refer to Infectious Disease for complex management and immunodeficiency evaluation.
must_collect: oxygenation status (SpO2 and/or ABG PaO2/A–a gradient if available)
immunocompromised risk (HIV status, CD4 count if known, transplant, chronic steroids, malignancy/chemo)
current medications relevant to interactions (warfarin, ACEi/ARB, spironolactone, methotrexate)
steps: Explain suspected pneumocystosis and seriousness; assess severity and need for hospital/ED evaluation.
Complete allergy_check and contraindication_check prior to prescribing.
Prescribe TMP-SMX weight-based dosing for 21 days with renal adjustment and lab monitoring plan.
If moderate–severe hypoxemia, prescribe adjunctive systemic steroids with standard taper and note to start within 72 hours.
Refer to Infectious Disease for confirmation strategy, management, and prophylaxis planning.

steps: Record diagnosis of pneumocystosis and explain it to the patient.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 insomnia benchmark, the core required outcome is correctly identifying and documenting insomnia as the diagnosis; treatment/referral actions are not required at this difficulty.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the word "insomnia" and frames it as a sleep disorder characterized by difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening with daytime impairment.

Screens for common reversible contributors (sleep schedule/behavior, caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, GERD, nocturia, medication/substance effects) before concluding primary insomnia.

Assesses basic severity and pattern: onset/duration, frequency, sleep latency, awakenings, total sleep time, early morning awakening, and daytime consequences (fatigue, mood, concentration, safety/accidents).

Checks for red flags requiring alternative evaluation: symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome, mania/hypomania, suicidality, and substance use disorder.
Meets communication milestone: explains the diagnosis and includes the term "insomnia" in patient-facing language.
Meets communication milestone: explains treatment options and includes both "CBT-I" as first-line and that "sedatives" may be considered short-term in selected cases.

Performs required safety checks BEFORE any prescribing discussion that becomes a plan to prescribe: verifies medication allergies (allergy_check), reviews current meds/supplements and interaction risk (drug_interaction_check), and evaluates contraindications (contraindication_check) such as pregnancy, older age/fall risk, respiratory disease/OSA risk, substance use disorder, hepatic impairment, and concomitant CNS depressants.

Avoids unsafe prescribing: does not recommend sedatives as first-line, does not suggest long-term sedative use, and warns about next-day impairment, falls, driving risk, and avoiding alcohol/other CNS depressants if sedatives are used.
Provides basic sleep hygiene and CBT-I components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation) and sets expectations for timeline of improvement.
Uses clear, nonjudgmental communication and invites questions; confirms patient understanding of diagnosis and plan.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after brief evaluation excluding common secondary causes and red flags; provide education and first-line management advice without prescribing.
must_collect: difficulty falling asleep and/or staying asleep
duration and frequency of sleep problem
steps: Elicit sleep complaint details (onset, pattern, frequency, sleep latency/awakenings, total sleep time, daytime impact).
Review sleep habits and contributors (sleep schedule, naps, screens, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine, exercise timing).
Screen for secondary causes and red flags (OSA, RLS, mood disorder/mania, substance use, pain/medical issues, medication-induced insomnia).
Explain diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and summarize why other urgent causes are less likely based on history.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line; mention sedatives as optional short-term adjunct only if needed, with safety cautions.
Document the diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose insomnia and recommend CBT-I plus sleep diary; discuss (but do not initiate) possible short-term sedative use contingent on completing safety checks and shared decision-making.
steps: Assess insomnia symptoms, duration, and daytime impairment.
Screen for OSA/RLS and mood/substance contributors.
Educate on insomnia and initiate CBT-I principles; recommend a 1–2 week sleep diary.
Discuss that sedatives exist but are not first-line; outline risks and that allergy/interaction/contraindication checks are required before any prescription.
Record insomnia diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose insomnia with emphasis on identifying medication/substance contributors and advising targeted changes plus CBT-I referral resources (apps/classes) without specialist referral.
steps: Review medication list and substances that can worsen sleep (stimulants, steroids, decongestants, caffeine, alcohol).
Screen for OSA/RLS and psychiatric contributors.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and provide CBT-I-focused plan and resources.
Mention sedatives as a possible short-term option only after required safety checks if later needed.
Record insomnia diagnosis.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old woman. You come in saying: “I can’t stop feeling on edge and my heart keeps racing, like something bad is about to happen.” Be vague and a bit evasive; give short answers unless asked follow-ups.

Volunteer early: nervous/tense most days for months, sudden pounding heart with shakiness, poor sleep with racing thoughts, constant thirst/peeing (you have diabetes insipidus; missing meds keeps you up all night), and that your last A1c was higher.

Only if asked: chest tightness/short breath, sweating/nausea/lightheadedness during spikes, health worries (stroke/passing out), weight gain embarrassment, headaches with light sensitivity, and a family member with severe headaches/one-sided weakness.

Withhold unless specifically probed: skipping meals, heavy caffeine use, checking pulse/BP repeatedly, avoiding crowds/meetings due to bathroom fear, poor focus, binge drinking water fast, taking extra doses of your DI medicine, alcohol at night, passive thoughts like “I can’t keep living like this” but no plan.

Express misconceptions: fear it’s diabetes, seizures, brain tumor, or heart disease.

If met with empathy, soften slightly but stay worried; emphasize cost concerns and preference for the cheapest tests/meds and minimal follow-ups.

required_args.reason: Confirm CVID diagnosis, evaluate vaccine antibody responses/immune phenotype, rule out secondary causes, initiate/monitor Ig replacement and complications (autoimmunity, bronchiectasis, GI disease, malignancy risk)
required_args.urgency: routine to expedited depending on infection severity/complications

tool_call_sequence.description: Ensures definitive diagnostic confirmation, comprehensive immune workup, and longitudinal specialist management for CVID and its systemic complications.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "common variable immunodeficiency" and "low IgG" in patient-appropriate language.
Explains the treatment plan explicitly as "immunoglobulin replacement" and states its goal (reducing infections/preventing complications).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing and documents/acknowledges the result; failure is critical.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., prior anaphylaxis to immunoglobulin, known anti-IgA with severe reactions, severe renal disease/high thrombotic risk considerations) and documents/acknowledges the result; failure is critical.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical) or explicitly states that no clinically significant interactions are expected but still reviews the medication list.
Avoids recommending live vaccines if significant antibody deficiency is suspected/confirmed; if vaccines are discussed, advises specialist guidance.
Provides clear safety-netting: when to seek urgent care (e.g., fever, sepsis symptoms, severe respiratory distress) and discusses infection-prevention measures.
Uses shared decision-making on IVIG vs SCIG (route, frequency, access, lifestyle) and discusses common adverse effects and monitoring needs.
Does not overpromise cure; frames CVID as chronic and manageable with ongoing therapy and follow-up.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose CVID based on low IgG and compatible clinical picture, start immunoglobulin replacement after required safety checks, and refer to immunology for confirmation and longitudinal management.
must_collect: history of recurrent/severe infections (sinopulmonary, otitis, pneumonia, GI infections)
documented low IgG (and ideally IgA/IgM if available)
medication history for secondary causes (e.g., rituximab, immunosuppressants, anticonvulsants)
history of protein loss (nephrotic syndrome, protein-losing enteropathy) or malignancy
infusion product allergy history and prior reactions to blood products/immunoglobulin
renal disease history and thrombotic risk factors
steps: State working diagnosis: common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) in setting of low IgG and compatible infection history; note need to exclude secondary causes.
Explain diagnosis to patient including the phrases "common variable immunodeficiency" and "low IgG" and what that means for infection risk.
Before prescribing, complete allergy_check and contraindication_check (critical) and document results; perform drug_interaction_check (non-critical).
Prescribe immunoglobulin replacement (IVIG or SCIG) with standard starting dose range and counseling on adverse effects and monitoring.
Refer to Allergy/Immunology for confirmation (vaccine antibody responses, B-cell phenotyping), dosing/titration, and evaluation for complications (bronchiectasis, autoimmunity, GI disease).
Create a follow-up expectation verbally (even if not a tool): monitoring infections, IgG troughs, adverse effects, and urgent return precautions.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records CVID as probable and explicitly documents exclusion of secondary hypogammaglobulinemia as part of the referral rationale while still initiating Ig replacement due to high clinical burden.
steps: Record diagnosis as probable CVID with low IgG; mention differential/secondary causes to be ruled out by specialist.
Explain diagnosis using required terms and discuss chronic nature and infection risk.
Complete allergy and contraindication checks before prescribing; review medication list for interactions.
Prescribe immunoglobulin replacement with route discussion (IVIG vs SCIG) and adverse effect counseling.
Refer to Immunology with emphasis on confirmatory testing and management of complications.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent chooses SCIG preferentially due to patient preference/venous access issues and documents appropriate counseling while meeting all safety checks and referral requirements.
steps: Record diagnosis: CVID with low IgG and recurrent infections.
Explain diagnosis with required terms.
Perform allergy_check and contraindication_check (critical) and drug_interaction_check (non-critical) prior to prescribing.
Prescribe SCIG with standard equivalent dosing framework and training/administration counseling.
Refer to Immunology for confirmation, dose titration, and monitoring.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes discussion of adjunct measures (vaccination strategy under specialist guidance, airway clearance if bronchiectasis suspected, prompt antibiotics for infections) without changing required outcomes.
steps: Record diagnosis: CVID supported by low IgG and infection history.
Explain diagnosis and treatment using required phrases.
Complete required safety checks prior to prescribing.
Prescribe immunoglobulin replacement and counsel on infection prevention and when to seek urgent care.
Refer to Immunology for comprehensive evaluation and long-term management.
